sources_audited.note: The 2026 O*NET occupation page supports the cited title-examiner population and the stated mix of document examination, summarization, record entry, compliance review, and problem resolution. It does not measure an industry wage share or AI substitution, which the PROXY bridge expressly acknowledges.

The CFPB page supports that title services include title insurance, title search, and often the closing-agent fee; that consumers can generally shop for these services; that lenders influence the available providers; and that closing roles vary by state. It supplies qualitative support rather than numeric eligibility or retention rates, consistent with the ESTIMATE labels.

The cited regulation defines title service to include title examination and evaluation, commitment and policy preparation, clearance of underwriting objections, administrative processing, and settlement. It supports service scope but not the numeric e, q, rho, or o values, which are labeled ESTIMATE.

The May 21, 2026 ALTA page reports $18.5 billion of 2025 title-insurance premium volume, up 13.8% from 2024, and connects the increase with mortgage originations. The packet correctly discloses that premium dollars are not constant-quality NAICS service quantities.

The 2026 ALTA index contains the cited statements that algorithms can scan and flag property-record data at scale while experienced human judgment remains important, and it describes state compliance guides covering all 50 states and the District of Columbia. The packet discloses the source's industry-association perspective.

The July 18, 2024 announcement confirms the acquisition of Brennan Title Abstract and reports an earlier Plymouth Group acquisition in June 2023. It demonstrates that transfers occur but provides no representative denominator, as disclosed.

The 2025 ALTA index reports the cited acquisitions of Texas Tradition Title, Delta South Title, Meurer Abstract & Title Company, and LEER Title Services. These selected announcements support occurrence, not the estimated five-year transfer rate, which is correctly labeled ESTIMATE.
The cited MBA PDF returned an internal access error when reopened, so the stated 2025-2028 mortgage-origination figures could not be verified.

caveats: S8 could not be reopened, leaving its mortgage-origination figures unverified. This is not material under the prescribed test: d5 is already labeled PROXY, S4 independently supports the recent directional relationship, and downgrading or removing S8's evidentiary support alone would not change the MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The numeric bases for e, s5, q, rho, and o are judgments rather than measurements. Their sources support service scope, competitive conditions, transfer occurrence, and implementation constraints, but not the selected percentages; the ESTIMATE labels, ranges, caveats, and change-evidence statements make that treatment honest.

The d5 evidence is based on nominal premium and mortgage-origination dollars, has a forecast horizon ending before the requested five years, and does not directly measure the broader NAICS service quantity. The PROXY bridge states these limitations and gives a plausible upward-bias direction.

S6 and S7 establish that agencies are acquired but are selected announcements with no eligible-firm denominator, so they do not independently substantiate the s5 base of 0.15; the record appropriately presents s5 as a broad ESTIMATE.
summary: Publishable with caveats. Mechanics pass, the lens is coherent and disclosed, evidence states are honest, factor constructs are not reversed or double counted, and the conclusion follows from the registered evidence. The record remains useful as an estimate-conditioned screen, but one cited forecast could not be verified and the key numeric primitives remain judgment-heavy.
